attributes.stem: A 40-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of intermittent retrosternal pain. He experiences several episodes a week when lying in bed and after consuming alcohol. Over-the-counter antacid preparations have given him limited symptomatic relief. His medical history includes hypertension and he takes perindopril. He does not take any other regular medications. He eats mostly take-away food and leads a sedentary lifestyle. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history and drinks about two bottles of wine a week. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His BMI is 33 kg/m2. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable. His uncle had gastric cancer at age 70 years, and his mother had breast cancer in her 60s.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial plan of management after lifestyle modifications?
options: Urea breath test for Helicobacter pylori infection
Over the counter antacids after meals regularly for 2 weeks
attributes.explanation:
Based on the patient's symptoms, medical history, and lack of concerning findings on physical examination, the most likely diagnosis is gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD). This is becoming an increasingly common problem in western communities, where poor dietary habits, obesity and high consumption are important aetiological factors. Given the patient's history and symptoms, a trial of lifestyle management and omeprazole 20 mg is the initial step in managing GERD. Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) that can help reduce acid production and alleviate symptoms. This approach is often effective and safe, and it's a reasonable initial strategy before considering invasive tests like endoscopy.

However, if any red flag symptoms are present, then a more active approach, including an endoscopic examination of the upper digestive tract, should be considered. Such symptoms might include pain or difficulty in swallowing, weight loss, increasing tiredness (anaemia), vomiting and age over 50. The family history of cancers does not put him at higher risk of gastric cancer given the age of onset, it is less likely to be hereditary.

Urea breath test for Helicobacter pylori antigen is not necessary at this time, as the patient's symptoms are suggestive of GORD and not of an H. pylori infection. H. pylori antigen would be useful if evaluating for abdominal pain that could be related to peptic ulcer disease, but that is not likely to be the case as described here.

Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy may be considered if the patient had red flag symptoms or, in the scenario described here, a failure to response to a positive change in lifestyle or a lack of response to acid-suppression medications. However, in this case there is no indication for such form of intervention at this stage of management.

Over the counter antacids after meals regularly for 2 weeks would be insufficient for this patient who has already tried antacids with limited relief. Having it regularly would not reduce symptoms. Antacids provide only temporary symptomatic relief and do not address the underlying cause of GORD. Given the chronicity and frequency of his symptoms, a more effective acid-suppressing medication like a PPI is indicated.

Oesophageal manometry is a diagnostic test used to evaluate esophageal motility disorders and is not the first-line test for GORD. While it can be useful in specific cases, such as when surgery is being considered or when the diagnosis is uncertain, it is not the most appropriate initial step in this patient's presentation.

It is important to make sure the patient has follow-up planned as the GP will want to make sure the patient's symptoms have responded to PPI therapy. If not, this would also indicate the need for endoscopy.

Regarding lifestyle modifications, evidence shows that weight loss has a dose-dependent association with reduction of GORD symptoms. A reduction in body mass index of 3.5 kg/m2 can result in nearly a 40% reduction in the risk of having frequent symptoms. Other helpful modifications include elevation of the head of the bed, avoiding meals 2-3 hours before bedtime for nocturnal symptoms, avoiding specific food that trigger symptoms, cessation of tobacco and alcohol (though evidence for symptom improvement is mixed). Medications with anticholinergic or smooth muscle-relaxing properties may worsen reflux, as may drugs causing chemical oesophagitis. If over-the-counter antacids or H2-receptor antagonists provide relief, they can be continued alongside lifestyle changes, with PPIs added short-term when symptoms persist and affect quality of life.
learningPoints: Lifestyle modification and adjustment is an essential initial component in the management of patients with gastro-oesophageal reflux disease.
Proton pump inhibitors are first-line pharmacological therapy for gastro-oesophageal reflux disease when lifestyle modifications alone are insufficient.
Weight loss has a dose-dependent association with reduction of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease symptoms.
references: Antunes C, Aleem A, Curtis SA. Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. [Updated 2022 Jul 4]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.

Keung C, Hebbard G. The management of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease. Australian Prescriber [Internet]. 2016 Feb 1;39(1):6–10. Available from: https://australianprescriber.tg.org.au/articles/the-management-of-gastro-oesophageal-reflux-disease.html
